name: Sacramento County, California
tagline: California's capital county and a top-tier urban-county buyer: a centralized Department of Technology (DTech) runs IT for county departments and regional partners, with a ~$9B budget under cost pressure shaping near-term tech spend.
overview: Sacramento County is the local government for California's capital region — roughly 1.6 million residents — and one of the larger urban-county technology buyers in the state. IT is centralized: the Department of Technology (DTech) is the county's internal service provider for infrastructure, applications, networking, telecom, and security across county departments, with the CIO also serving as the County's Chief Information Security Officer. DTech is best understood as a shared-services shop that delivers to the county's operating departments rather than a standalone agency with its own large discretionary spend — the dollars sit with the departments it serves (Health, Human Assistance, Sheriff, Assessor, Voter Registration, etc.). The county runs a recommended budget of roughly $9B for FY2026-27, but that budget is under real pressure: a projected nine-figure structural deficit, $57M in general-fund reductions, and ~194.5 positions cut. For a Dell SLG seller, that combination — a centralized IT buying center plus a cost-constrained budget — means the motion leans toward consolidation, refresh efficiency, and modernization that demonstrably lowers run-cost, routed through the county's central procurement office.

budget.intro: Sacramento County's FY2026-27 Recommended Budget is about $9B all-funds (roughly $4B general fund), approved by the Board of Supervisors on June 10, 2026, with a Revised Recommended Budget due for adoption in September. The headline for tech buyers is the cost pressure behind it: a structural deficit (county officials cited ~$101M, about half attributed to federal changes), $57M in general-fund reductions, and ~194.5 FTE deletions. IT spend isn't broken out as a single public line — it flows through DTech's internal cost recovery and the budgets of the departments it serves.

stats.label: County budget (FY26-27)
stats.note: All-funds Recommended Budget approved June 10, 2026; ~$4B general fund

stats.label: Projected deficit
stats.note: Structural gap cited by county; roughly half tied to federal funding changes

notes: DTech is funded largely through internal service charges to the departments it supports, not a single large general-revenue IT appropriation.
Budget pressure favors modernization with a clear run-cost or efficiency case; net-new discretionary spend will be scrutinized through September adoption.

initiatives.name: Property tax system modernization (TaxSys)
initiatives.summary: The county replaced a 1980s-era mainframe property-tax platform — which handles $3.1B+ in annual tax revenue across schools, cities, and special districts — with a modern system (TaxSys), live as of fall 2025. A reference point for the county's appetite to retire legacy and a model for the next mainframe-to-modern push.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & CISO function
initiatives.summary: The CIO doubles as County CISO, consolidating security under DTech. Security assessments, penetration testing, and resilience are recurring procurement themes — a durable entry point for endpoint, identity, and infrastructure-security offerings even in a tight budget.

initiatives.name: Infrastructure & data-center refresh
initiatives.summary: As a centralized shared-services provider, DTech carries ongoing server, storage, network, and end-user compute refresh across county departments. In a cost-constrained year, the pitch is consolidation and lifecycle efficiency, not greenfield expansion.

initiatives.name: Cloud & AI evaluation
initiatives.summary: Like most California counties, Sacramento is weighing cloud and emerging AI use cases within governance and budget limits. Treat AI as early-stage and tie any proposal to a concrete department workflow and a defensible cost case rather than a broad platform play.

procurement.intro: Selling to Sacramento County runs through its central procurement office — the Department of General Services, Contract & Purchasing Services Division (CAPSD) — which issues solicitations (increasingly via an online procurement portal). Two paths shorten the cycle: the county's cooperative program and California statewide leveraged agreements that the county can buy off without a fresh RFP.

vehicles.name: County DGS / Contract & Purchasing Services (CAPSD)
vehicles.detail: The central buying office for county solicitations, terms, and vendor registration — the front door for direct county business.

vehicles.name: Sacramento County Procurement Alliance (SCoPA)
vehicles.detail: The county's cooperative-contract program — a competitively awarded vehicle other public agencies can also buy from, broadening the reach of a single award.

vehicles.name: California statewide LPAs (CMAS / Cal eProcure)
vehicles.detail: California Multiple Award Schedules and other DGS leveraged procurement agreements let the county buy off pre-competed statewide contracts — confirm Dell's CMAS/LPA coverage is active and correctly scoped.

powerStructure.name: David Villanueva
powerStructure.title: County Executive
powerStructure.note: Top of the county administration and budget; sets the cost-discipline frame every department (including DTech) operates within.

powerStructure.name: Rami Zakaria
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer (and County CISO)
powerStructure.note: Owns DTech, county IT strategy, and security policy — the central technology relationship for the account.

powerStructure.name: Assistant CIO / Chief Technology Officer
powerStructure.title: DTech technology leadership
powerStructure.note: Deputy/CTO role advising the CIO; the county has run recruitment for this seat — confirm the current incumbent before mapping the buying center.

powerStructure.name: Dept. of General Services — Contract & Purchasing Services
powerStructure.title: Central procurement (CAPSD)
powerStructure.note: Owns solicitations, vendor onboarding, and the cooperative/LPA pathways — the procedural gatekeeper for any deal.

powerStructure.name: Board of Supervisors
powerStructure.title: Five-member governing board
powerStructure.note: Approves the budget and major contracts; the body that adopts the final spending plan in September.

news.title: Board approves ~$9B FY2026-27 Recommended Budget amid deficit and cuts
news.impact: Cost pressure ($57M GF reductions, ~194.5 positions) shapes near-term IT spend toward efficiency and modernization; final adoption lands in September.

news.title: County cites ~$101M structural deficit, ~half tied to federal funding changes
news.impact: Discretionary tech spend will be tightly scrutinized — lead with run-cost reduction and consolidation cases, not net-new platforms.

news.title: County launches modern property tax system (TaxSys), retiring 1980s mainframe
news.impact: Proof point that the county will fund legacy retirement when the case is strong — a template for the next modernization conversation.

news.title: DTech publishes FY2025-26 IT Plan
news.impact: The public roadmap for county IT priorities and upcoming solicitations — a planning anchor for timing the account.

dellActions: Anchor the relationship with CIO/CISO Rami Zakaria and DTech as the central buying center, and confirm the current Assistant CIO/CTO incumbent before mapping the org.
Lead with run-cost reduction and consolidation — refresh, infrastructure efficiency, and lifecycle value that survives a $57M general-fund-cut year.
Use the TaxSys legacy retirement as the proof point for the next modernization play; frame proposals around a concrete department workflow and a defensible cost case.
Verify Dell's CMAS / California LPA coverage and explore SCoPA so the county can buy without a fresh RFP; register and track solicitations through CAPSD.
Time the motion around the September Revised Recommended Budget adoption and the DTech IT Plan cycle when priorities and funding firm up.
